coredata.dc:title: Preparation of pH-sensitive chitosan-magnetic graphene quantum dot bionanocomposite hydrogel beads for drug delivery application: Emphasis on effects nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Poursadegh, Hossein
Barzegarzadeh, Mehdi
Amini-Fazl, Mohammad Sadegh
coredata.dc:description: Chitosan-based bionanocomposite hydrogel beads have attracted much attention as carriers in drug delivery systems due to their inherent properties, such as excellent biocompatibility, high swelling, and high storage capacities. In this study, a combination of chitosan hydrogel with different amounts of MGQD was prepared and used for pH-sensitive controlled release of MTX. The successful synthesis was confirmed through various analysis methods. The fabricated CS, CS-MGQD 5%, CS-MGQD 10%, and CS-MGQD 15% hydrogel beads loaded about 84, 83, 77, and 64% of MTX, respectively. The results of the swelling behavior and drug release behavior showed that the hydrogel beads experience pH-dependent swelling and release of MTX. In addition, investigating the effect of MGQD concentration on swelling behavior and drug release showed that CS-MGQD 15% has favorable stability and controllable drug release in an acid environment. Also, the Weibull kinetic model was the best-fitting model for MTX release from CS-MGQD 15% bionanocomposite hydrogels at pH 5. These findings suggest that the prepared magnetic bionanocomposite hydrogel beads have good potential for implantable pH-sensitive drug delivery in the treatment of cancerous tissue.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bionanocomposite
Hydrogel beads
Chitosan
Magnetic Graphene quantum dot
Drug delivery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of bionanocomposite CS-MGQD hydrogel beads and methotrexate drug loading
In-vitro drug loading and release studies

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors express their sincere gratitude to the University of Tabriz for providing financial. Not applicable. Not applicable. The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author, upon reasonable request.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of MGQD formation and loading of MTX and coating with chitosan.
A) ft-ir spectra of cs (a), mgqd (b), cs-mgqd 5% (c), cs-mgqd 10% (d), and cs-mgqd 15% (e); b) xrd patterns of cs (a), mgqd (b), cs-mgqd 5% (c), cs-mgqd 10% (d), and cs-mgqd 15% (e).
Magnetic hysteresis loops for CS-MGQD 5%, CS-MGQD 10%, and CS-MGQD 15% hydrogel beads.
SEM images of Fe3O4 (a), MGQD (b), pure CS bead (c,d) CS-MGQD 15% bead (e,f), and MTX/CS-MGQD 15% (g,h).
EDX-mapping images for CS-MGQD 15% bead (a), and MTX/CS-MGQD 15% (b).
Swelling behavior of MTX/CS, MTX/CS-MGQD 5 %, MTX/CS-MGQD 10 %, and MTX/CS-MGQD 15 % in pH = 5 (a) and pH = 7.4(b).
Drug loaded behavior of CS, CS-MGQD5%, CS-MGQD10%, and CS-MGQD15%.
Drug release behavior of MTX/CS, MTX/CS-MGQD 5 %, MTX/CS-MGQD 10 %, and MTX/CS-MGQD 15 % in pH = 5 (a) and pH = 7.4(b).
The result of drug release kinetics.

head.ce:title: Preparation of pH-sensitive chitosan-magnetic graphene quantum dot bionanocomposite hydrogel beads for drug delivery application: Emphasis on effects nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Polymer Material Research Laboratory, Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Polymer Material Research Laboratory, Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a combination of chitosan hydrogel with different amount of MGQD was prepared and used for pH-sensitive controlled release of MTX. Hydrogel beads prepared displayed pH-responsive swelling and controlled release behavior. The bio-nanocomposites demonstrated a high loading capacity for MTX. By increasing the amount of MGQD nanoparticles, the drug release rate improved.

Chitosan-based bionanocomposite hydrogel beads have attracted much attention as carriers in drug delivery systems due to their inherent properties, such as excellent biocompatibility, high swelling, and high storage capacities. In this study, a combination of chitosan hydrogel with different amounts of MGQD was prepared and used for pH-sensitive controlled release of MTX. The successful synthesis was confirmed through various analysis methods. The fabricated CS, CS-MGQD 5%, CS-MGQD 10%, and CS-MGQD 15% hydrogel beads loaded about 84, 83, 77, and 64% of MTX, respectively. The results of the swelling behavior and drug release behavior showed that the hydrogel beads experience pH-dependent swelling and release of MTX. In addition, investigating the effect of MGQD concentration on swelling behavior and drug release showed that CS-MGQD 15% has favorable stability and controllable drug release in an acid environment. Also, the Weibull kinetic model was the best-fitting model for MTX release from CS-MGQD 15% bionanocomposite hydrogels at pH 5. These findings suggest that the prepared magnetic bionanocomposite hydrogel beads have good potential for implantable pH-sensitive drug delivery in the treatment of cancerous tissue.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a complex and diverse disease that can be deadly and debilitating. The primary approaches used to treat cancer typically involve a combination of various methods, such as surgery, radiation therapy, chemotherapy, immunotherapy, targeted therapy, and hormone therapy [1,2]. Getting drugs to the right place in tumors has been a major challenge in the quest for long-term remission and a cure. Researchers have focused on finding ways to boost the concentration of therapeutic agents in tumors to improve treatment outcomes. Drug delivery systems (DDSs) play a crucial role in improving the efficacy of medications, reducing unwanted side effects, and ensuring the safe delivery of drugs [3,4]. The values of pH are significantly different in various diseased conditions such as infections, ischemia, inflammations, and cancers, as well as in different tissues or organs. In recent years, several smart polymer systems responsive to pH have been widely used as carriers for drug delivery systems [5–7].

Chitosan (CS) is one of the most abundant natural biopolymers and is widely used as a suitable carrier in drug delivery systems due to its beneficial properties like being an immune enhancer, nontoxic, pH-sensitive, antifungal, antiulcer, antacid, antitumor activity, biodegradability, biocompatibility, etc. Chitosan, in the form of hydrogel beads, is a three-dimensional network structure that is capable of absorbing large amounts of water and has attracted much attention as a drug-controlled release. However, the low mechanical strength of chitosan hydrogel beads limits the application of chitosan beads as a carrier in drug delivery systems [8]. To overcome these problems, the introduction of nanoparticles including silicon or carbon materials [4,9], graphene quantum dots [10], graphene oxide [11], and iron oxide can increase the stability of chitosan beads and provide a more continuous and slow release mode of drugs [12].

In recent years, there has been extensive research conducted on the utilization of iron oxide nanoparticles (Fe3O4) as carriers within drug delivery systems. Recent studies propose that the integration of magnetic and nanostructures offers two significant benefits. Firstly, it ensures biocompatibility, making it compatible for utilization in biomedical fields. Secondly, it enables precise drug delivery to designated sites with the aid of an external magnetic field [13–16]. In addition, iron oxide nanoparticles aggregate in an aqueous solution due to their high surface area and dipole–dipole interaction, which reduces the drug delivery capacity [17,18]. To overcome this problem, graphene quantum dots nanoparticles are used as a protective and stabilizing layer to prevent direct contact between iron oxide nanoparticles. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with unique properties such as chemical stability, high biocompatibility, low toxicity, photoluminescence, and excellent thermal and mechanical stability properties have recently attracted attention in the field of drug delivery [19–21]. In addition to these properties, the introduction of magnetic quantum dot graphene (MGQD) to chitosan beads could lead to a more continuous and slow-release mode of drugs. The drug Methotrexate (MTX) was chosen as a model due to its smaller size compared to the pores of Synthesis MGQD. MTX is commonly prescribed as a first-generation anticancer drug for various types of cancers, including osteosarcoma, leukemia, cervical and breast cancer, and blood malignancies, as well as for treating rheumatoid arthritis [22,23]. While the clinical application of MTX in cancer treatment is extensively documented, its oral administration is often restricted due to challenges in pharmacokinetics, significant side effects, susceptibility to patient drug resistance, and the requirement for high dosages [24,25]. To solve the aforementioned issue with the oral delivery of MTX, a recent solution has emerged in which the drug is encapsulated within biocompatible hydrogel beads composed of polymers [26]. Therefore, in this study, the model drug was loaded into an MGQD nanocomposite (MTX/CS-MGQD). For example, rGO/Fe3O4/CS nanocomposite was used as targeted drug delivery for the doxorubicin drug [27]. In another study, PPy/mSiO2/GQDs were recently used as a temperature-sensitive carrier for the release of the drug MTX [28].

The current research involved the preparation of a chitosan hydrogel composite with different amounts of MGQDs for application as an attractive and pH-sensitive drug carrier. The bio nanocomposite hydrogel was crosslinked using a NaOH solution, followed by the loading of chitosan hydrogel beads containing MTX (Fig. 1 ). The performance of drug delivery was assessed through experiments focusing on drug encapsulation and release under laboratory conditions. Additionally, the study explored the effect of MGQD on swelling behavior and drug release.

The chitosan was purchased from East Azerbaijan, Iran. Merck Chemical Co. supplied iron(II) chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2 4H2O), iron(III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3 6H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ammonia (NH3), acetic acid, ethanol, N,N-Dimethylformamide(DMF), sodium acetate, and monopotassium phosphate (KH2PO4), all of which were of analytical grade. Methotrexate (MTX) was provided by Cipla India Co. in these experiments, and all solvents and other materials used were obtained from Merck Co.

The KBr tablets were used to obtain Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra in the 3700–400 cm−1 range with Bruker Instruments (Tensor 27, Germany). The samples were analyzed using UV–VIS absorption spectra using an Analytik Jena (Specord 250) German instrument. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed at room temperature using a Tongda (TD-3700) China X-ray diffractometer with a scan range of 2θ 10°–70°. The chemical composition of scanning electron microscopy (SEM) pictures and Energy Dispersive X-ray Analysis (EDX) and elemental mapping (MAP) techniques by running a TESCA-scanning electron microscope at 30 kV. An experimental magnetization test was conducted using a vibrating sample magnetometer VSM, Magnet Kavir Ardabil, in a magnetic field of −125,00–125.00 Oe at room temperature.

To synthesize Fe3O4 nanoparticles, a solution containing 2 g of FeCl3 6H2O and 1 g of FeCl2 4H2O was initially prepared and stirred in water for 20 min. Next, the mixture was heated at 80 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere, and 5 mL of NH3 was gradually introduced to create the desired reaction environment. After 30 min, the iron nanoparticles that formed were separated using an external magnetic field and washed with distilled water.

GQD was prepared using the pyrolysis of citric acid. Then, 1 g of Fe3O4 was dispersed in 50 mL of distilled water containing 0.02 g/mL GQD for 30 min. The resulting mixture was stirred at room temperature for 12 h. Finally, the obtained brown product was separated from the solution with an external magnet, washed several times with distilled water, and then dried at 60 °C [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of bionanocomposite CS-MGQD hydrogel beads and methotrexate drug loading
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogel beads-based nanocomposites, i.e., CS, CS-MGQD 5 %, CS-MGQD 10 %, and CS-MGQD 15 %, were synthesized according to our previously reported methods [29]. MTX loading on CS, CS-MGQD 5 %, CS-MGQD 10 %, and CS-MGQD 15 % nanocarriers was carried out by mixing CS-MGQD aqueous suspension with 0.1 g of MTX. Briefly, 1 g of CS and the desired amount of MGQD (0.0, 0.05, 0. 1, and 0. 15 g) were added to 40 mL of MTX solution (0.1 g), then 1 mL of acetic acid was added and stirred until a uniform solution was obtained. Then, a uniform solution of MTX-CS-MGQD was added dropwise in an aqueous solution (100 mL) containing NaOH (10 g) at 25 °C to form MTX/CS-MGQD hydrogel beads. Then the seeds were separated with an external magnet, washed several times with distilled water, and dried at 50 °C. In this manuscript, the loading of MTX in bionanocomposite hydrogel beads was coded as MTX/CS, MTX/CS-MGQD 5 %, MTX/CS-MGQD 10 %, and MTX/CS-MGQD 15 %, respectively.

To study the swelling properties of the beads, the immersing method was employed. The hydrogel beads were tested for swelling in two buffer solutions with pH values of 7.4 and 5. To perform the test, 10 mg of the hydrogels were weighed and immersed in 2 mL of the buffer solutions at room temperature. The swollen beads were then separated from the buffer solution at specific intervals, and the water adsorbed on the bead's surface was removed with filter paper and immediately weighed. The percentage of swelling was calculated using Eq. (1).
(1)
S w e l l i n g
r a t e
( % )
=
( W s - W d )
Wd
×
100
The weight of the samples after swelling at a specified time is represented by Ws, and the weight of the initial dried samples is represented by Wd.

To examine how drug release is affected by changes in pH, 10 mg of hydrogels loaded with the drug were immersed in 10 mL of PBS at two different pH values, 7.4 and 5. At specific time intervals, a 2 mL sample of the aqueous solution containing the drug was removed and replaced with a fresh buffer. The capacity load and cumulative release were recorded by comparing the collected data to a pre-established calibration curve for each drug. The amount of drug that was released was determined using Eq. (2).
(2)
D r u g
r e l e a s e
( % )
=
t h e
a m o u n t
o f
r e l e a s e d
d r u g
The
a m o u n t
o f
l o a d e d
d r u g
× 100

Various mathematical and semi-empirical models were employed to comprehend the mechanisms of MTX release. Seven traditional kinetic models were employed to assess the mechanisms of MTX release, and the equations for these models are listed in Table 1 . The equations contain variables such as Qt, which refers to the cumulative amount of MTX released at time t, Q0, which is the initial amount of MTX released; K0, which is the zero-order constant; K1, which is the first-order constant; Ks1, and Ks2, which are the second-degree polynomial model release-rate constants; KH, which is the Higuchi constant; and Qt/Q∞, which is the fractional release of MTX. Additionally, Kkp refers to the Korsmeyer-Peppas constant, kd1 and kd2 represent the constant diffusion relaxation, and n is the diffusional exponent. In the Weibull model, n represents the drug release curve and determines its mechanism, while kb is proportional to the initial explosive release rate, and a higher kb value corresponds to a larger initial explosive release.

FT-IR spectra of CS, MGQD, CS-MGQD 5 %, CS-MGQD 10 %, and CS-MGQD 15 % are displayed in Fig. 2 a. In the FT-IR spectrum of CS, a broad band at 3434 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of the –OH and –NH2 groups. The peaks at 1590 cm−1 and 1626 cm−1 could be assigned to the bending of the –NH2 group and the stretching of the CO amide group, respectively [30,31]. The FT-IR spectrum of MGQD indicates the characteristic peaks at 3452 cm−1 (OH stretching), 1586 cm−1 (CO stretching), 1390 cm−1 (–CH2 symmetric stretching), and another sharp peak at 589 cm−1 is attributed to the vibration of the Fe-O stretching band [32]. As expected, for CS-MGQD bionanocomposite hydrogel beads, almost all peaks of CS and MGQD are observed with a significant shift, which may be attributed to the intermolecular electrostatic interaction between CS and MGQD. Also, the FT-IR spectra of CS-MGQD 5 %, CS-MGQD 10 %, and CS-MGQD 15 % are very similar to each other (some minor changes in the intensity of peaks), which shows that the nanocomposites hydrogel beads with different amounts of MGQD have a similar structure.

The XRD results of CS, MGQD, CS-MGQD 5 %, CS-MGQD 10 %, and CS-MGQD 15 % are displayed in Fig. 2b. In XRD analysis of CS, the peaks at 2θ = 10.85° and 20.3° belong to the crystal planes of (020) and (110), respectively [33]. The XRD pattern of MGQD shows the diffraction peaks at 2θ = 26.1◦, 30.25◦, 35.7◦, 43 ◦, 57.5◦, and 63◦, which correspond to (002), (220), (311), (400), (511), and (440) crystal planes, respectively [34]. In the XRD analysis of CS-MGQD 5 %, CS-MGQD 10 %, and CS-MGQD 15 %, almost all the diffraction peaks of CS and MGQD are detected, which confirms the successful formation of CS-MGQD bionanocomposite hydrogel beads. Also, by increasing the MGQD content in the CS, the intensity of the peaks related to MGQD increases.

The magnetic properties of the samples were measured using a Vibrating Sample Magnetometer (VSM). The result of this test revealed that each sample had magnetic characteristics. It was also discovered that the magnetic property increased as the amount of MGQD in the nanocomposites increased, which may be related to the rise in the proportion of iron nanoparticles. The findings demonstrated that nanocomposite CS-MGQD 15 % had a higher magnetic property (2.87 emu/g) than the other samples, which allowed it to travel toward the magnet and respond favorably to the Presence of an external magnetic field (Fig. 3 ).

The morphology of the Fe3O4, MGQD, CS, and CS-MGQD 15 % were studied using FE-SEM, as illustrated in Fig. 4 . The SEM image of Fe3O4 clearly shows that the iron oxide has a spherical shape (Fig. 4a). Fig. 4b shows the layer structures with spherical particles, which can be attributed to the homogeneous distribution of Fe3O4 on the layers of GQD sheets. SEM images of CS, CS-MGQD 15 %, and MTX/CS-MGQD 15 % hydrogel beads at low and high magnifications are displayed in Fig. 4c-h. The SEM image of CS hydrogel beads exhibited a porous and rough microstructure. A comparison of the SEM images of CS and CS-MGQD 15 % hydrogel beads showed that the addition of MGQD to the matrix of CS led to changes in the morphology of the CS hydrogel beads and increased wrinkled areas. These physical properties cause good swelling of the hydrogel beads and qualify the CS-MGQD 15 % as a promising carrier for effective drug loading and release process. This is because there are many hydrophilic functional groups, including –COOH and –OH on the MGQD surface. This leads to a strong interaction (hydrogen bonding) of the hydrogel beads with water molecules and thus a higher adsorption capacity [35,36].These physical properties cause good swelling of the hydrogel beads and qualify the CS-MGQD 15 % as a promising carrier for an effective drug loading and release process. This is because there are many hydrophilic functional groups, including –COOH and –OH on the MGQD surface. This leads to an intermolecular interaction between MGQD and chitosan chains, which can result in a dense structure of the hydrogel beads.

The chemical structures of CS-MGQD 15 % and MTX/CS-MGQD 15 % were confirmed by elemental analysis. The EDX spectrum further characterized C, O, N, Na, and Fe elements in CS-MGQD 15 % hydrogel beads. After loading MTX on CS-MGQD 15 %, the intensity of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), iron (Fe), and oxygen (O) peaks in the EDX spectrum of MTX/CS-MGQD changed slightly. (Fig. 5 ).

The swelling behavior of the synthesized hydrogels was studied in two different environments to simulate physiological and cancer tissue conditions. Specifically, the hydrogels were tested in PBS (pH = 7.4, 37 °C) to mimic the physiological environment and in SAB (pH = 5, 41 °C) to simulate the cancer tissue environment, as depicted in Fig. 6 . In both cases, it was observed that all samples exhibited time-dependent swelling. Additionally, the swelling behavior was pH-dependent, with higher swelling ratios observed at acidic pH levels. In general, the swelling was higher at pH 5. At this pH, the amine groups of chitosan were protonated, resulting in greater electrostatic repulsion within the polymer network, which led to an increased tendency to swell. The chitosan hydrogel beads degrade after 10 h of exposure to a buffered solution with a pH of 5, which causes the swelling to gradually decrease until it is zero. The presence of positive acetate ions in the buffered solution, which attacks the crosslinked chitosan network and leads to protonated and network disintegration, accounts for the reduction in swelling after 10 h [37]. Conversely, as the pH increased to 7.4 and physical cross-linking occurred through hydrogen bonding interactions between the functional groups of chitosan glucose units, the polymer network tended to shrink [38]. As the percentage of nanoparticles increased, the swelling decreased. This can be attributed to the electrostatic and hydrogen interactions that occur, which result in greater cross-linking of the polymer. Additionally, it can be observed that as the percentage of nanoparticles increases and they penetrate the cavities of the chitosan hydrogel, the swelling of the hydrogel decreases. Furthermore, since the cross-linking action of the hydrogels was physical, the hydrogels decomposed after some time at pH 5 [36,39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-vitro drug loading and release studies

ce:section.ce:para: The study investigated the rate at which MTX is released from different types of beads, namely MTX/CS-MGQD 5 %, MTX/CS-MGQD 10 %, and MTX/CS-MGQD 15 %, under different pH conditions. The collected experimental data were analyzed, and the correlation coefficient (R2) values for all models were compared in Table 2 . The aim was to find the most appropriate model that accurately described the release process. By assessing the correlation coefficient for each carrier in both pH 5 and 7.4 environments, the optimal model was determined, showing the mechanism of drug release. How MTX is released from the carriers MTX/CS-MGQD 5 %, MTX/CS-MGQD 10 %, and MTX/CS-MGQD 15 % in an acidic environment follows the diffusion relaxation model, where Kd1 and Kd2 are the penetration and comfort coefficients, respectively. Importantly, the lower value of Kd2 compared to Kd1 for these nanocomposites suggests that the drug release mechanism is predominantly governed by Fickian diffusion [43]. Furthermore, in the case of spherical matrices, a value of n below 0.43 indicates that the drug release mechanism from the nanocomposite follows Fickian diffusion. If the value is between 0.43 and 0.85, it suggests non-Fickian diffusion, while higher values exceeding 0.85 indicate case II transport. In this study, as the value of n was below 0.43 for all samples, it can be concluded that the dominant release mechanism of the MTX drug was Fickian diffusion. [44,45]. Moreover, the release mechanism of the drug from three nanocomposites in the alkaline environment obeyed the Weibull model. The kb parameter in the Weibull equation shows the release characteristics of the drug's primary explosive. Significantly, the MTX/CS-MGQD 15 % nanocomposite displayed a lower kb value in both acidic and alkaline conditions, indicating a decreased initial burst of the drug. Based on these findings, it can be inferred that the MTX/CS-MGQD 15 % nanocomposite offers the most optimal choice for attaining controlled drug release [46].

In this study, a combination of chitosan hydrogel beads with different amounts of MGQD (CS-MGQD) was prepared and applied as a carrier for methotrexate delivery. Various analyses, such as FT-IR, XRD, SEM, and EDX, confirmed the correctness of the synthesis. Based on VSM analysis, it was found that CS-MGQD 15 % nanocomposite hydrogel beads can respond to the external magnetic field and move toward the target location. The fabricated bionanocomposite hydrogel beads were loaded with MTX, and its drug delivery performance was assessed through drug encapsulation and in vitro drug release studies. The CS, CS-MGQD 5 %, CS-MGQD 10 %, and CS-MGQD 15 % hydrogel beads demonstrated high pH sensitivity in releasing the MTX drug at pH 5 compared to pH 7.4. Furthermore, the effect of the amount of MGQD on swelling behavior and drug release was investigated. Upon examining the drug release of the nanocomposites, it was observed that the MTX/CS-MGQD 15 % nanocomposite exhibited low release in the physiological environment (pH = 7.4) of the body while demonstrating the highest drug release and controlled drug release in the acidic environment (pH = 5). Consequently, the MTX/CS-MGQD 15 % nanocomposite was chosen as the optimal nanocomposite.

Hossein Poursadegh: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Visualization, Software, Writing – original draft, Project administration. Mehdi Barzegarzadeh: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation. Mohammad Sadegh Amini-Fazl: Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express their sincere gratitude to the University of Tabriz for providing financial.

ce:section.ce:para: Not applicable.
Not applicable.
The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author, upon reasonable request.
